summary.category: India's Monsoon Climate and Seasonal Patterns
points: India's monsoon climate is defined by seasonal reversal of wind systems — north-easterly dry winds in winter (Nov-Feb) and south-westerly moisture-laden winds in summer (Jun-Sep) — creating four distinct seasons with extreme regional variations in temperature (50°C in deserts to -45°C in Kashmir) and rainfall (400+ cm in Meghalaya to <10 cm in Rajasthan).

Six climatic controls shape India's climate: latitude (Tropic of Cancer dividing tropical and subtropical zones), altitude (Himalayas blocking Central Asian cold), pressure and wind systems (monsoon reversal), continentality (extreme interior variations), ocean currents, and relief features (rain shadow effects); the Himalayas and coastal moderating influences create temperature contrasts between interior and coastal regions.

The monsoon acts as a unifying force across India — its seasonal rhythm determines the agricultural calendar, water availability, and livelihood patterns for millions, binding the entire nation through a rhythmic cycle of wet and dry spells, despite its uncertainties and uneven distribution that cause both floods and droughts in different regions.
